title: Lal Mohan (Deep-Fried Sweet Dumplings in Sugar Syrup)

ingredients.title: For the Dough
items: 2 tbsp semolina (sooji/rava) – optional, for slight crunch
2 tbsp milk powder – optional, for richness
2 tbsp ghee (clarified butter) or unsalted butter

ingredients.title: For the Sugar Syrup
items: A few saffron strands (optional, for color and aroma)
1 tsp rose water or kewra water (optional)

ingredients.title: For Frying
items: Ghee or vegetable oil, enough for deep frying

instruction.title: Prepare the Dough
steps: In a large bowl, combine flour, semolina, milk powder, baking powder, baking soda, and salt.
Rub in ghee until the mixture looks like coarse crumbs.
Add yogurt little by little and knead into a soft, slightly sticky dough. Add water sparingly if needed.
Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 15–20 minutes.

instruction.title: Make the Sugar Syrup
steps: In a saucepan, combine sugar and water, bring to a boil.
Add cardamom pods and saffron. Simmer until syrup reaches a slightly sticky, one-string consistency.
Turn off heat and add rose water/kewra water if using. Keep warm.

instruction.title: Shape and Fry the Lal Mohan
steps: Divide dough into small even-sized balls (~1 inch).
Heat ghee or oil in a deep pan on medium-low.
Tip: Frying too hot will brown the outside but leave the inside raw.
Fry the balls in batches, gently stirring to ensure even browning, until golden brown and puffed. Remove and drain on paper towels.

instruction.title: Soak in Sugar Syrup
steps: While still warm, immerse the fried Lal Mohans into the warm sugar syrup.
Let them soak for 10–15 minutes until fully absorbed and soft.

instruction.title: Serve
steps: Serve warm or at room temperature.
Garnish with slivered pistachios or almonds if desired.

chefTips: Always fry on medium-low heat; high heat makes the exterior hard.
Use slightly sticky dough for soft, airy texture.
Resting the dough allows proper gluten formation for puffiness.
Soak while warm for maximum syrup absorption.
Optional: Add a tiny pinch of red food color to the syrup for that classic deep red “restaurant” look.
